does: This paper measures how large language models affect the landscape of N-day exploits—vulnerabilities known to developers but not yet patched across all systems. Through systematic red-team evaluation, the authors test whether frontier LLMs can assist in discovering, understanding, and weaponizing unpatched vulnerabilities at scale. They establish benchmarks for LLM-assisted exploit development, quantifying both the speed and success rate at which models can generate working payloads, craft bypasses, and identify attack surfaces for publicly known but unpatched vulnerabilities. The work provides empirical data on the realistic threat posed by LLM-augmented attackers against the typical patch deployment timeline.
connects: This research builds on the growing body of work evaluating LLMs' capabilities in cybersecurity contexts, extending earlier studies on code generation and vulnerability analysis to the practical threat model of N-day exploitation. It responds to concerns raised in prior work about dual-use capabilities of large models and connects to the literature on responsible disclosure, patch economics, and the security implications of information asymmetry between defenders and attackers. The paper advances beyond theoretical discussions of LLM misuse by providing concrete measurements, complementing previous red-teaming efforts focused on jailbreaking, malware generation, and social engineering.
enables: The findings inform defensive priorities and vulnerability management strategies, highlighting the urgency of patching cycles and the need for monitoring systems that detect LLM-assisted attack patterns. The work enables future research into detection mechanisms for automated exploitation, accelerated patching frameworks, and security practices resilient to machine-augmented attackers. It also supports policy discussions around responsible AI deployment, informing decisions about access controls on frontier models and the design of vulnerability disclosure programs that account for LLM-mediated risk amplification in the N-day window.
